Yusuf Hamied Department of Chemistry, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, United Kingdom Marine Biological Laboratory Chromatin Collaborative, Marine Biological Laboratory, Woods Hole, MA 02543, USA Department of Physical-Chemistry, Universidad Complutense de Madrid, Av. Complutense s/n, 28040, Madrid, Spain Institute for Research in Biomedicine (IRB Barcelona), The Barcelona Institute of Science and Technology, Barcelona 08028, Spain Department of Mathematics and Computer Science, University of Barcelona, Barcelona 08007, Spain Department of Biophysics and Howard Hughes Medical Institute, University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, TX, USA Department of Genetics, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, United Kingdom Department of Biochemistry and Biomedicine, University of Barcelona, Barcelona 08028, Spain (Dated: November 17, 2025) Understanding how chromatin’s physicochemical properties shape its emergent organisation is central to deciphering genome function. To address this, we present OpenCGChromatin, a high-performance coarse-grained model that achieves near-atomistic simulations of chromatin systems an order of magnitude larger than previously possible, spanning biomolecular condensates and fibers tens of kilobases in length. OpenCGChromatin simulations independently predict, from physicochemical principles, the linker-DNA-dependent chromatin structures observed by cryo-ET and the relative thermodynamic stability of condensates inferred from biochemical assays. Crucially, OpenCGChromatin resolves histone-tail dynamics and interaction networks that remain inaccessible experimentally, explaining how linker-DNA length controls histone tail accessibility and the resulting multiscale structure of chromatin condensates. Extending simulations to 108-nucleosome fibers shows that acetylation disrupts chromatin compaction in a pattern-specific manner by weakening key tail-mediated interactions, with H4K16 and H3K9 emerging as the most energetically disruptive modifications. These results position OpenCGChromatin as a powerful framework for linking molecular detail to emergent chromatin organization.

key determinants of this process. In particular, the structure of individual chromatin fibers tunes collective phase behav80 ior [8, 9, 26]. Linker lengths close to integer multiples of the 81 DNA helical repeat plus 5 bp (i.e., 10N+5 bp, where N is an 82 integer) promote disordered chromatin configurations that 83 enhance multivalent interactions and favor phase separa84 tion, whereas linkers at integer multiples (i.e., 10N bp) form 85 more ordered structures that suppress it [8, 9, 26, 27]. The 86 disordered organization of nucleosomes—facilitated by nu87 cleosome breathing—further amplifies chromatin multiva88 lency, increasing the density of molecular connections within 89 the condensed chromatin liquid network and reinforcing the 90 stability of its condensates [25]. Understanding how chromatin conformational dynamics, 92 and the ensuing formation and breakage of intermolecular 93 interactions, regulates the properties of chromatin conden94 sates is crucial for uncovering the physical principles that or95 ganize the genome and regulate its function. Yet, capturing 96 these processes across the relevant molecular and mesoscale 97 regimes remains a major challenge. State-of-the-art cryo98 ET can now visualize chromatin organization inside conden99 sates at nucleosome resolution [9], but it cannot directly 100 resolve the dynamical behavior of individual biomolecules 101 and quantify the energetic determinants of phase separa102 tion. Conversely, existing computational models either ne103 glect chemical specificity or lack the resolution and efficiency 104 needed to connect residue-level interactions with mesoscale 105 organization [24]. To bridge this gap, computational frame106 works must simultaneously capture the chemical detail of 107 histones and DNA and the emergent mesoscale behavior of 108 chromatin across phase boundaries. Among available ap109 proaches, so-called near-atomistic coarse-grained models— 110 where individual amino acids and DNA base pairs are explic111 itly represented—offer this potential [25, 41, 42]. However, 112 their high computational cost and the rugged energy land113 scapes of chromatin have so far restricted simulations to 114 small arrays of 3–12 nucleosomes, precluding direct explo115 ration of gene-scale chromatin organization and condensate 116 thermodynamics. Here, we introduce OpenCGChromatin, a high118 performance, residue- and nucleotide-resolution coarse119 grained model that achieves over an order-of-magnitude ac120 celeration relative to our previous residue-level model [25] 121 while improving physicochemical accuracy and experimen122 tal agreement (Fig. 2, Fig. S3). This breakthrough ex123 tends the frontiers of near-atomistic simulations to dynam124 ics of hundreds of nucleosomes and biomolecular conden125 sates. The model recapitulates key experimental observa126 tions, such as the oscillatory dependence of chromatin com127 paction on linker DNA length [6, 7], the structural motifs 128 resolved by cryo-EM [43], X-ray crystallography [44] and 129 cryo-ET [9], and the modulation of chromatin condensate 130 stability with linker DNA length [8, 9]. OpenCGChromatin 131 provides, for the first time, full access to the dynamical con132 figurational ensembles of chromatin, and its histone tails, 133 across phase boundaries at near-atomistic resolution, re134 vealing the molecular and thermodynamic mechanisms by 135 which phase separation emerges and is regulated. In doing

so, it bridges chemical-specific molecular interactions with mesoscale structure and thermodynamics. Using OpenCGChromatin, we find that chromatin phase 139 separation arises from the interplay of linker DNA length 140 and mechanics, anisotropic internucleosome interactions, 141 histone-tail dynamics, and chemical specificity. Linkers 142 close to 10N+5 introduce single-fiber structural frustra143 tion that prevents regular nucleosome stacking, limits fiber 144 compaction, and increases chromatin multivalency, thereby 145 promoting phase separation. This frustration exposes nu146 cleosome faces and histone tails to solvent, promoting the 147 formation of highly-connected intermolecular networks sta148 bilized by enthalpic and entropic contributions. In con149 trast, 10N linkers favor compact zigzag conformations with 150 internally saturated faces that sequester H4 and H2A N151 tails, suppressing inter-fiber connectivity and preventing 152 macroscopic phase separation. This behavior deviates from 153 the conventional flexible sticker-and-spacer polymer model, 154 where longer repulsive spacers increase conformational free155 dom and promote polymer expansion. OpenCGChromatin 156 thus provides a physically grounded, near-atomistic descrip157 tion of the mechanisms regulating chromatin phase behav158 ior, establishing a molecular foundation for understanding 159 how phase separation contributes to genome organization.

II.

A.

RESULTS

The OpenCGChromatin model

We introduce OpenCGChromatin, a high-performance coarse-grained chromatin model that bridges atomistic de164 tail and mesoscale organization, enabling simulations of 165 chromatin arrays spanning tens of kilobases and conden166 sates containing hundreds of nucleosomes at near-atomistic 167 resolution. OpenCGChromatin constitutes a complete GPU169 compatible reformulation of our previous CPU-only 170 chemically specific chromatin coarse-grained model, which 171 represented represented histones at residue resolution and 172 DNA at the base-pair–step level using ellipsoids. The new 173 framework uses a fully particle-based description of histones 174 and DNA, with one bead per amino acid (centered at the ′ 175 Cα ) and one per nucleotide (centered at the C1 position) 176 plus three virtual sites per base pair, while treating solvent 177 and ions implicitly [45]. The entire interaction scheme was 178 redesigned and reparameterized using a hybrid bottom-up 179 and top-down approach. At the bottom-up level, bead 180 sizes, charges, interaction terms, configurational potentials, 181 and nucleosome configurations were derived from atomistic 182 structures and simulations [4, 18, 25, 45] to preserve 183 essential chemical and structural features of chromatin 184 components. At the top-down level, the energetic balance 185 between electrostatic and hydrophobic protein–protein, 186 protein–DNA and DNA–DNA interactions were tuned to 187 reproduce experimental trends in fiber compaction with 188 respect to linker length [6, 7] (Fig. S4). This comprehensive 189 redesign ensures that OpenCGChromatin quantitatively

reproduces experimental observables across molecular and mesoscale regimes. A central advance of OpenCGChromatin is the replace193 ment of the rigid base pair description of DNA—based on 194 helical coordinates and an ellipsoidal representation—with 195 the state-of-the-art CGeNArate coarse-grained model [45], 196 which employs a fully particle-based Cartesian represen197 tation. CGeNArate is a bottom-up coarse-grained model 198 for B-form DNA parametrized from large-scale all-atom 199 molecular dynamics simulations at the tetranucleotide level, 200 while also capturing emergent mechanical properties of long 201 DNA oligomers. In our previous chromatin model [25], 202 representing inter-basepair DNA deformations in helical 203 space required nontrivial coordinate transformations and 204 Jacobians to compute forces. In contrast, CGeNArate 205 treats nucleotides as Cartesian particles whose local de-

formations are described by low-order, sequence-dependent polynomial functions, enabling seamless integration with 208 OpenMM [46, 47]. In addition, unlike the symmetric rigid 209 base pair model, CGeNArate explicitly resolves the major 210 and minor grooves of B-form DNA, providing a more faith211 ful description of DNA shape, geometry, and flexibility. As 212 a result, CGeNArate accurately reproduces the equilibrium 213 atomistic free-energy landscape of B-form DNA, including 214 twist–bend coupling and sequence-dependent stiffness, and 215 captures the dynamics of diverse DNA constructs, including 216 circular duplexes, kilobase-length fragments, and even com217 plete mitochondrial DNA [45]. In OpenCGChromatin, the 218 one-bead-per-nucleotide of CGeNArate is augmented with 219 three additional virtual sites per base pair: two phosphate 220 beads carrying negative charges for electrostatic interac221 tions, and one excluded volume bead positioned at the helix

[Page 4]

center to prevent unphysical histone tail configurations such as penetration into the DNA helix. The secondary structure of the globular domains of the 225 histone proteins and the overall architecture of the histone 226 octamer are maintained using elastic network models de227 rived from atomistic simulations of nucleosomes [16], as in 228 our previous work [25]. Histone tails are treated as fully 229 flexible polymers. The geometry of the nucleosomal DNA 230 around the histone protein core is taken from high-resolution 231 crystal structures of nucleosomes [4]. The central 127 bp 232 of nucleosomal DNA are included in the elastic network to 233 maintain single-nucleosome stability, while allowing breath234 ing motions in the terminal 10 bp on each flank [3]. More 235 extensive nucleosome breathing, which may occur in na236 tive chromatin arrays [48], could be explored in future work 237 by relaxing these restraints. Detailed model architecture, 238 Hamiltonian, parameterization strategy, and comparison to 239 our previous chemical-specific coarse-grained model [25] are 240 provided in Supplementary Information. To assess the computational efficiency of this 242 GPU-accelerated implementation, we benchmarked 243 OpenCGChromatin on systems ranging from 12 to 108 244 nucleosomes. The implementation of OpenCGChromatin in 245 OpenMM achieved performance gains exceeding an order 246 of magnitude over previous CPU-based models (Fig. 1b). 247 Benchmarking on both consumer (NVIDIA RTX 4080) 248 and data-center (NVIDIA H100) GPUs revealed that for 249 108-nucleosome systems (∼190,000 particles), a single 250 H100 GPU outperformed a 128-core AMD EPYC 7742 251 node by more than an order of magnitude (Fig. 1b). Even 252 for smaller 12-nucleosome fiber simulations, we observed 253 9-fold speedups relative to a full 56-core Intel Cascade 254 Lake CPU node (Fig. 1b). When single simulations do not 255 saturate the GPU, NVIDIA’s Multi-Process Service can be 256 used to run multiple independent simulations concurrently, 257 further increasing total throughput (Fig. 1b). Importantly, 258 these comparisons juxtapose one GPU against many tens 259 or hundreds of CPU cores; the per-core acceleration, and 260 associated reductions in computational cost and energy 261 consumption, are therefore even more striking. Thus, the 262 real-world gains in throughput and resource efficiency for 263 large-scale OpenCGChromatin simulations are substantially 264 greater than even the raw order-of-magnitude improve265 ments suggest. This high-performance model makes it 266 possible to simulate chromatin organization and bulk phase 267 behavior at near-atomistic resolution and scales that were 268 previously unattainable.

B.

Model Validation

To validate OpenCGChromatin, we compare its structure and compaction predictions for 12-nucleosome arrays with 272 different regular linker DNA lengths with in vitro exper273 iments on reconstituted chromatin fibers, including sedi274 mentation velocity analyzes (Fig. 2a) [6, 7], and structures 275 obtained by cryo-EM [43], X-ray crystalography [44] and 276 cryo-ET (Fig. S7) [9].

We simulated ten distinct 12-nucleosome arrays with regular linker lengths equal to 18, 20, 22, 25, 30, 36, 41, 53, 279 58 and 63 bp. To ensure exhaustive sampling of the rugged 280 free-energy landscape underlying chromatin at high resolu281 tion, we employed our Debye-length Hamiltonian replica282 exchange (HREX) protocol [25]. In this method, replicas at 283 constant temperature exchange configurations across differ284 ent values of the Debye length (λD ) governing the screened 285 Coulomb interactions. Fifteen HREX replicas were dis286 tributed between λD = 8.0 Å (corresponding to 150 mM 287 NaCl) and λD = 13.0 Å (corresponding to 60 mM NaCl), 288 achieving an average exchange acceptance of approximately 289 30 % (Supplementary Information, Fig. S7). This approach 290 is particularly efficient for chromatin systems, requiring only 291 about one-fifth as many replicas as conventional tempera292 ture replica exchange, as the dominant free-energy barri293 ers arise from electrostatic interactions [25]. Moreover, be294 cause the Debye lengths correspond to experimentally rel295 evant salt concentrations, HREX simultaneously enhances 296 sampling and enables direct probing of the salt-dependent 297 conformational behavior of chromatin. The sedimentation coefficient (S) is widely used as a 299 low-resolution, ensemble-average metric of chromatin com300 paction, with higher values indicating more compact con301 formations [6]. Results from the HREX simulations show 302 that OpenCGChromatin achieves near-quantitative agree303 ment with experimental sedimentation coefficients at phys304 iological salt (150 mM). The model accurately captures 305 the characteristic oscillatory dependence of chromatin com306 paction on linker DNA length [6], where arrays with linker 307 lengths close to 10N bp are the most compact and those 308 near 10N+5 bp are the least compact (Fig. 2a). This 309 agreement is particularly significant as, to our knowledge, 310 no previous near-atomistic chromatin model, including our 311 original chemically specific model (Supplementary Informa312 tion, Fig. S3), has reproduced this fundamental experimen313 tal trend. In addition, comparing fiber compaction via the 314 radius of gyration, as predicted by the simulations and mea315 sured by cryo-ET at 150 mM for 25 bp and 30 bp fibers, 316 shows that all cryo-ET values fall within the simulated dis317 tributions (Fig. S7a). Chromatin compaction emerges from a balance between 319 DNA–DNA electrostatic repulsion, anisotropic nucleosome– 320 nucleosome interactions, and DNA mechanics. This balance 321 depends sensitively on linker length and ionic environment. 322 As the linker DNA becomes longer, the total negative charge 323 of chromatin increases. Therefore, as DNA lengthens, the 324 electrostatic repulsion between adjacent DNA segments be325 gins to dominate over attractive histone-mediated inter326 actions and DNA mechanical constraints. This shift pro327 gressively drives chromatin towards decompaction at long 328 linker lengths, regardless of the 10N versus 10N+5 peri329 odicity. Consistent with this picture, at low ionic strength 330 (60 mM NaCl), diminished electrostatic screening amplifies 331 DNA–DNA repulsion, promoting less compact chromatin 332 states. OpenCGChromatin accurately reproduces this salt333 dependent decompaction for short to medium linker DNAs 334 (Fig. 2a). For the longest linkers (≥50–63 bp), the ex277

Because nucleosome–nucleosome interactions are intrin350 sically anisotropic, we characterized chromatin structures 351 across linker lengths by quantifying inter-nucleosome con352 tact frequencies as a function of neighbor order (ith neigh353 bors) and relative orientation, classifying them into face– 354 face, face–side, and side–side categories. Here, the face de355 notes the flat nucleosome surface containing the H2A–H2B 356 acidic patch, while the side refers to its cylindrical periph357 ery, perpendicular to the face plane. We have previously 358 shown that face–face stacking yields the strongest inter359 nucleosome interactions, followed by face–side and side–side 360 contacts [8, 25]. Hence, face–face contacts are enthalpically 361 preferred, provided nucleosomes do not need to disturb the 362 orientations imposed by their linker DNAs. In Fig. 2b, we show the contact probability for different 364 classes of second-nearest-neighbor nucleosome interactions 365 as a function of linker length, highlighting the prevalence 366 of face–face interactions for linker lengths close to 10N bp 367 and face–side interactions for those close to 10N+5 bp. 368 Face–face and total second-nearest-neighbor contacts dis369 play an oscillatory dependence on linker length, diminishing 370 as DNA lengthens mirroring the compaction behaviour in 371 Fig. 2a. Fig. 2c displays fractions of the total nucleosome– 372 nucleosome interactions in each system, allowing compar373 ison of the relative importance of different contact types. 374 Fig. S9 provides the underlying interaction counts normal375 ized only by the number of frames, enabling comparison of 376 absolute interaction levels across linker lengths. Our analysis confirms that the oscillatory compaction 378 trend in our simulations arises for the correct physical rea379 son: the helical twist of B-form DNA, which completes one 380 full turn every ∼10.5–10.7 bp [50], determines the fraction 381 of second-nearest-neighbor face–face stacking interactions 382 that drive compaction (Fig. 2b). Thus, in agreement with 383 experiments [9, 43, 44], linker lengths close to integer multi384 ples of the helical repeat (10N bp) position adjacent nucleo◦ 385 somes in phase—with their dyad axes rotated by roughly 0 386 relative to one another—allowing second-nearest neighbors 387 to align for optimal face–face stacking, yielding the charac388 teristic compact zigzag architecture (Fig. 2c). In contrast, 389 as shown experimentally [9], linker lengths of 10N+5 bp ◦ 390 correspond to roughly one and a half DNA turns (a 180 391 rotational offset between nucleosomes), placing them out 392 of phase and thereby suppressing face–face contacts. When 393 compared to the 10N structures, the 10N+5 fibers adopt 394 more expanded heterogeneous structures with a greater pro395 portion of face–side contacts (Fig. 2c) [8, 9]. The total 396 number of inter-nucleosome interactions per fiber decreases 397 progressively, consistent with the greater decompaction ob398 served at longer linker lengths (Fig. S9). To quantitatively compare the structures predicted by 400 OpenCGChromatin with those visualized by cryo-ET for 12343

nucleosome arrays with 25 or 30 bp linkers [9], we computed three geometric descriptors of consecutive nucleo403 some triplets and compared with the distributions reported 404 for cryo-ET fibers in our previous work [9]. These in405 clude the center-of-mass distance between second-nearest406 neighbors (D), and dihedral angles between nucleosome 407 planes αi,i+1 and parai,i+2 (Fig. S7b). Here, αi,i+1 describes 408 the relative orientation of sequential nucleosome planes and 409 parai,i+2 reports the orientation of second-nearest-neighbor 410 nucleosome planes (details of calculations in Supplementary 411 Information). The simulated D and αi,i+1 closely match 412 those from cryo-ET, while parai,i+2 shows the correct trend 413 but with a slightly higher mean for 25 bp and a broader dis414 tribution for 30 bp linkers. The broader distribution likely 415 reflects the more exhaustive sampling of inter-nucleosome 416 configurations in the simulations compared with cryo-ET. 417 Larger parai,i+2 values for 25 bp indicate that second418 nearest-neighbor nucleosomes are slightly more out of phase 419 in the simulations than in the experiment. This discrepancy 420 could arise from modest over-rigidity in linker-DNA torsion 421 and bending, or overestimation of DNA–DNA electrostatic 422 repulsion under the symmetric Debye–Hückel treatment (as 423 discussed for compaction and in previous work [49]). Be424 cause parai,i+2 depends on the relative orientation of nucleo425 somes connected by two consecutive linkers, it is more sensi426 tive than D or αi,i+1 to small variations in linker-DNA twist 427 and bending. Twist deviations accumulate over two link428 ers, amplifying minor differences in torsional rigidity, helical 429 pitch, or electrostatic interactions. Nonetheless, the overall 430 agreement is excellent. Indeed, consistently with the predic431 tions of OpenCGChromatin, analysis of the cryo-ET internu432 cleosome interactions [9] confirms the dominance of ordered 433 face–face arrangements for 10N-like linkers and the preva434 lence of irregular configurations with face–side interactions 435 for 10N+5-like arrays. Given that high-resolution structural 436 data for 10N+5 chromatin remain scarce, this agreement 437 provides rare structural insight into the less-characterized, 438 irregular regime. The 10N chromatin structures also align with the more 440 widely available high-resolution molecular data [43, 44], 441 with ordered zigzag configurations matching cryo-EM struc442 tures of reconstituted 12-nucleosome arrays (30 bp linker) 443 and the tetranucleosome crystal structure (20 bp linker). Regardless of the 10N vs 10N+5 periodicity or linker 445 length, second-nearest neighbor (i ± 2) interactions con446 sistently dominate, in agreement with in situ sequencing447 based assays—including Micro-C [51, 52], RICC-seq [53], 448 and Hi-CO [54]—which resolve single-nucleosome interac449 tions across variable linkers and reveal preferential i ± 2 450 contacts. OpenCGChromatin complements experiments by unveil452 ing the molecular basis of the linker-length-dependent struc453 tural differences via analysis of residue-level DNA–protein 454 and protein–protein inter-nucleosome contacts. To gain molecular and thermodynamic insight into these 532 differences, we quantified the ‘network connectivity’ and 533 ‘valency’ of chromatin arrays across simulations [40]. The 534 network connectivity was defined as the number of arrays 535 in the largest connected component; that is, the largest set 536 of chromatin arrays linked through pairwise contacts. Two 537 arrays were considered connected if any pair of their nu538 cleosomes lay within a defined cutoff distance (see Supple539 mentary Information). The valency of each chromatin array 540 was quantified as the number of distinct arrays it contacted 541 within this cutoff. Consistent with their phase behavior, 542 25 bp chromatin forms a stable, condensate-spanning per543 colated network encompassing nearly all arrays (77–78 out 544 of 81), with individual arrays exhibiting high valency (fre545 quently 8–14 partners per array). In contrast, the 30 bp 546 chromatin displays low valency and transient inter-molecular 547 connectivity, with most arrays interacting with fewer than 548 four others and forming small clusters that continuously 549 form and dissolve (Fig. 4c,d). A key advantage of OpenCGChromatin is that it enable 551 us to zoom in on chromatin within and outside conden552 sates, connecting molecular structure with mesoscale phase 553 behavior. To probe how the conformational landscape of 554 chromatin changes across the phase boundary, and how 555 those changes, in turn, regulate condensation, we performed 556 additional single-array HREX simulations of 25 and 30 bp 557 tetranucleosome fibers (i.e., chromatin in infinite dilute con558 ditions). We then analyzed over 30,000 tetranucleosome 559 conformations from simulations probing both condensate560 forming and dilute conditions (see Supplementary Informa561 tion), representing the trajectories using symmetrized inter562 nucleosomal distance features [65]. In this representation, 563 each tetranucleosome conformation is described by a set of 564 six pairwise center-of-mass distances between nucleosomes 565 (d12 , d13 , d14 , d23 , d24 , d34 ). To account for the approxi566 mate symmetry of the tetranucleosome arrays (i.e., where 567 nucleosomes 1 and 4 are equivalent at the edges of the fiber, 568 and nucleosomes 2 and 3 are equivalent at the center), a 569 symmetrized version of these features was used. For exam570 ple, d12 and d34 are combined into s1 = d12 + d34 (see Sup571 plementary Information). Although chromatin is not intrin572 sically symmetric along the 5’–3’ direction of the DNA, the 573 coarse-grained representation is nearly symmetric with re574 spect to fiber reversal, and the energetic differences between 575 the two directions are negligible at the resolution considered 576 here. Thus, symmetrization reduces noise and ensures that 577 conformational clustering robustly captures structural vari578 ability. To characterize the conformational landscape, we 579 applied k-means (k = 3) clustering to the combined en580 semble, identifying three intuitive states: compact (C1, two 581 nucleosome pairs in contact), semi-extended (C2, one pair 582 in contact), and extended (C3, no nucleosome–nucleosome 583 contacts) (Fig. 5a). Principal component analysis was then 584 used to reduce dimensionality for visualization, with the first 585 two components capturing over 77% of the overall variation

C.

Physical and molecular mechanisms of chromatin phase separation

We investigated the phase behavior of chromatin by performing, to our knowledge, the first direct coexistence 506 molecular dynamics simulations of chromatin solutions at 507 residue and nucleotide resolution. We simulated two types 508 of systems, each comprising 81 tetranucleosome fibers 509 with either 25 or 30 bp linker lengths, at room temper510 ature and 100 mM NaCl. Using the direct coexistence 511 method, we simulated the condensed and diluted phases 512 in the same elongated simulation box, separated by an in513 terface [64]. origin of the contrasting structural behaviour of 25 versus 30 bp linkers lies in the inter-nucleosome orientations 630 imposed by the linker DNA. Linkers of 10N+5 bp intro631 duce a half-helical turn rotational mismatch that frustrates 632 regular face–face stacking between nucleosomes, preventing 633 the formation of ordered zigzag fibers. This zigzag frus634 tration yields structurally flexible, dynamic fibers that can 635 open up in the dilute phase and reorganize within the con636 densate. Conversely, 10N linkers favor face–face stacking, 637 which sequesters nucleosome surfaces and constrains con638 formational rearrangements. Linker-DNA-imposed zigzag 639 frustration therefore acts to control phase behavior: 25 bp 640 fibers can expand to maximise inter-molecular interactions, 641 whereas 30 bp fibers remain locked in compact zigzag con642 formations. The difference in intra-array nucleosome stacking for 644 25 versus 30 bp systems has important consequences 645 for histone-tail accessibility and intermolecular connec646 tivity. Energy-weighted contact analyses reveal that in 647 30 bp chromatin the H4 and H2A N-terminal tails—the 648 strongest energetic contributors to nucleosome–nucleosome 649 interactions—are buried in intra-array face–face interfaces, 650 stabilizing folded fibers but suppressing inter-array binding 651 (Fig. 6). In contrast, in the more expanded 25 bp fibers, 652 all histone tails, including H4 and H2A N as well as H3 653 and H2A C, are exposed to the solvent and available to 654 mediate a diverse set of inter-fiber interactions, increasing 655 chromatin valency and the network connectivity of the con656 densate (Fig. 6). Thus, in 25 bp chromatin the interaction 657 energy, that is concentrated in H4/H2A N intra-array con658 tacts in 30 bp fibres, is redistributed across heterogeneous 659 interactions involving all the histone tails. The differential roles of histone tails in 25 versus 30 bp 661 condensates predicted from these unbiased OpenCGChro662 matin condensate simulations closely match our earlier re663 sults based on simulations also using OpenCGChromatin 664 but in which nucleosome positions were restrained to those 665 observed in the cryo-ET data [9]. That the same be666 haviour emerges independently—now in simulations with667 out any restraints and in the more demanding context of 668 sampling equilibrium condensate densities—provides strong 669 additional validation of the model. Altogether, these results suggest that chromatin phase 671 separation is governed not only by the balance of attractive 672 sticker (histone tail-mediated) and repulsive spacer (DNA– 673 DNA) interactions, but also by single-molecule conforma674 tional properties. Despite carrying a higher net negative 675 charge—and hence greater electrostatic self-repulsion—the 676 30 bp arrays fail to phase separate because they are locked in 677 compact zigzag structures that prevent reorganization and 678 multivalent engagement. In contrast, the simulations sug679 gest that linker-induced frustration in the 25 bp arrays en680 ables conformational expansion upon phase separation that 681 exposes additional interaction sites, allowing fibers to re682 organize and diversify their interaction modes. This struc683 tural adaptability increases both the number and variety of 684 crosslinks that sustain the condensate, providing a direct 685 coupling between single-fiber frustration, histone-tail acces-
sibility, and network connectivity that defines how chromatin’s geometry and chemical makeup jointly control its 688 phase behavior.

The distinct molecular interaction networks identified here—concentrated H4/H2A intra-array contacts in 30 bp 691 versus diverse inter-array tail-mediated crosslinks in 25 bp 692 condensates—suggest differences in macroscopic conden693 sate material properties that are not directly accessible from 694 our near-atomistic simulations. To probe these emergent 695 physical properties, we carried out direct coexistence sim696 ulations of tetranucleosome chromatin arrays with 25 and 697 30 bp linker lengths using our minimal coarse-grained model 698 for chromatin [25] (Fig. S10a,b), which approximates chro699 matin at lower resolution (1 bead per histone protein core 700 and 1 ellipsoid per 5 base pairs) and enables extensive sam701 pling for full binodals (Fig. S10c) and rheological calcula702 tions (Fig. S10d,e). Analysis of the shear stress relaxation 703 modulus, G (t), reveals that behavior on short time scales −7 704 (less than 10 s) is similar for both 25 and 30 bp chro705 matin, indicating that the fast, dynamic processes contribut706 ing to viscoelastic behavior on these timescales (primarily 707 intra-molecular fluctuations such as bond and angle relax708 ations) are similar in both cases. In contrast, the longer 709 time behaviour shows that G (t)25 bp > G (t)30 bp . Since 710 slower, collective mechanisms such as fiber rearrangement 711 and diffusion dominate viscoelastic behavior in the longer 712 time regime, we can infer that these processes are hindered 713 in the more dense, strongly connected condensates formed 714 by 25 bp chromatin (Fig. S10d). Furthermore, we find that 715 the surface tension of 25 bp chromatin is higher than that 716 of 30 bp chromatin, consistent with stronger intermolecu717 lar interactions (Fig. S10e). This concordance between the 718 molecular interaction networks revealed by OpenCGChro719 matin and the macroscopic material properties calculated 720 with the minimal model demonstrates that linker-DNA721 controlled structural frustration has direct, measurable con722 sequences for condensate rheology.

In summary, chromatin does not follow the conventional flexible polymer paradigm, where longer repulsive linkers are 725 expected to increase chain flexibility and promote polymer 726 expansion. Instead, linker-DNA length, through the me727 chanical constraints and nucleosome orientations it imposes, 728 shifts chromatin between two semi-flexible polymer regimes. 729 Arrays with 10N linkers form rigid zigzag structures in which 730 nucleosomes are locked into stable intra-array interactions, 731 limiting their ability to form inter-array connectivity. By 732 contrast, 10N+5 chromatin behaves as a more compliant 733 semi-flexible polymer, where disrupted intra-array stacking 734 restricts single-fibre compaction while enabling structural 735 irregularity that favours multivalent, tail-mediated inter736 array interactions. This structural adaptability enhances 737 inter-array multivalency and underlies the higher thermo738 dynamic stability of 10N+5 chromatin condensates relative 739 to 10N [8, 9, 26].

Histone acetylation modulates structure and dynamics of long chromatin fibers

Finally, we applied OpenCGChromatin to investigate how histone acetylation modulates chromatin structure and dy744 namics on the scale of small genes (over 18 kb). We con745 ducted 100 μs simulations of 108-nucleosome fibers with 746 regularly spaced 22 bp linkers and different distributions of 747 partially acetylated nucleosomes. Because neither the sto742

ichiometry nor the specific combinations of lysines acetylated per nucleosome within euchromatin are quantitatively 750 resolved in vivo, we randomly acetylated 50% of lysine 751 residues in the histone tails to explore the upper bound of 752 plausible acetylation density. We compared three systems: 753 (i) a 108-nucleosome control fiber in which all histones were 754 unmodified (wild-type, 0 Ac); (ii) a partially acetylated fiber 755 in which 12-nucleosome blocks of acetylated and unmodi756 fied nucleosomes alternated along the fiber (5×12 Ac), and

[Page 13]

(iii) a partially acetylated fiber in which the central 54nucleosome region was acetylated, flanked by two unmodi759 fied regions of 27-nucleosomes each (54 Ac) (Fig. 7a–c). We find that acetylated systems consistently adopt more 761 extended conformations with larger radius of gyration fluc762 tuations, whilst the unmodified fiber collapses into a com763 pact globule over around 40 μs (Fig. 7d). This behaviour 764 arises because the 108-nucleosome unmodified fiber consti765 tutes a polymer with very high effective sticker (i.e. nu766 cleosomes with histone tails) density. In the collapsed glob767 ule, nucleosomes can form many simultaneous tail-mediated 768 contacts with other nucleosomes, maximising the overall va769 lency of the system, and decreasing its free energy. Acety770 lated nucleosomes have fewer effective binding sites, and re771 duced valencies, and therefore they form instead elongated 772 fiber-like structures in which they predominantly interact 773 with their nearest neighbors. Crowding density analysis reveals that linker regions 775 flanked by acetylated nucleosomes have significantly lower 776 local densities than those flanked by unmodified nucleo777 somes, corresponding to higher DNA accessibility (Fig. 7a– 778 c). This increased accessibility provides a molecular mech779 anism for the well-established role of histone acetylation in 780 decompacting chromatin and promoting transcriptional ac781 tivity [66, 67]. Analysis of nucleosome–nucleosome contact frequencies 783 shows that acetylation produces a graded reduction in inter784 nucleosome contacts (Fig. 8b). Because partially acetylated 785 nucleosomes still form attractive interactions with other nu786 cleosomes, but with reduced strength, their contacts break 787 more readily and reform less frequently, leading to larger 788 average inter-nucleosome separations between neighboring 789 particles. However, by construction, the torsional and bend790 ing rigidity of the linker DNA constrain both the orientation 791 and the spacing of consecutive nucleosomes, effectively lim792 iting how far apart neighboring nucleosomes are relative to 793 one another. This geometric coupling maintains frequent 794 contacts between second-nearest neighbors (j = i ±2), even 795 though acetylation increases their mean separation. In con796 trast, for fourth neighbors (j = i ± 4), and even more so 797 for longer-range pairs (|i − j| > 4), the cumulative increase 798 in inter-nucleosome spacing over successive linkers cannot 799 be compensated by DNA rigidity. Long-range contacts are 800 then almost exclusively mediated by WT–WT pairs: the 801 WT–WT contact fractions for |i − j| > 4 are similar in 802 the 0 Ac and 54 Ac fibers, whereas the 5×12 Ac pattern 803 shows a strong depletion of such bridges and very few Ac804 containing long-range contacts. Thus, contiguous unacety805 lated stretches act as multivalent hubs that drive long-range 806 compaction, while intervening acetylated segments behave 807 as spacers that fragment these contact networks. Our re808 sults support the view that acetylation patterns are cru809 cial determinants of higher-order chromatin folding, and the 810 methodology we introduce offers a useful tool for probing 811 exactly how such patterning differences drive fiber folding. To identify which lysine sites are most affected by acetyla813 tion, we performed an energy-weighted residue-level contact 814 analysis across all 108-nucleosome fiber simulations. Unlike

a simple contact analysis that assigns a value of 1 when two beads are closer than a cutoff and 0 otherwise, this ap817 proach integrates the interaction energy between bead pairs 818 at their instantaneous separations, providing a measure of 819 the strength and frequency of residue–DNA and residue– 820 residue associations. For each lysine site (e.g., H4K16), we 821 quantified its mean contribution to inter-nucleosome inter822 action energy and compared cases where the residue was 823 acetylated versus unacetylated. To isolate the intrinsic en824 ergetic effect of acetylation, data from all three simulated 825 systems (0 Ac, 5×12 Ac, 54 Ac) were pooled and then 826 partitioned by lysine identity and acetylation state. These analyses highlight a subset of lysines whose acety828 lation most strongly weakens inter-nucleosome interac829 tions (Fig. 8c). Remarkably, our simple coarse-grained 830 model, using an unbiased approach of randomly acetylat831 ing 50% of tail lysines, successfully identifies H4K16 and 832 H3K9—two of the best-characterized regulatory acetylation 833 marks [9, 18, 60, 68, 69]—as having the largest energetic im834 pact. This agreement suggests that much of these sites’ reg835 ulatory importance stems from basic physicochemical princi836 ples: acetylating a lysine within a charge block disrupts elec837 trostatic interactions that cannot be compensated by non838 electrostatic contacts, as neighboring cations still preferen839 tially seek electrostatic partners. H4K16 (adjacent to R17) 840 and H3K9 (adjacent to R8) are particularly sensitive because 841 arginines cannot be acetylated, ensuring that acetylation of 842 the neighboring lysine always creates incompatible interac843 tion preferences within the charge block. Our analysis also 844 reveals substantial reductions in contact energy upon acety845 lation of the closely spaced H2AK13 and H2AK15, which 846 are ubiquitination targets involved in DNA repair [70] but 847 not established transcriptional regulators. One possibility is 848 that enzymatic acetylation of neighboring lysines may fa849 vor diacetylation over mono-acetylation, and while our ran850 dom 50% acetylation frequently generates mono-acetylated 851 states with conflicting interaction preferences, diacetylated 852 tails may retain a greater capacity for cohesive tail–tail in853 teractions. In contrast, lysines adjacent to non-acetylatable 854 arginines consistently disrupt inter-nucleosome contacts re855 gardless of local acetylation context, potentially explaining 856 their more prominent regulatory roles in chromatin com857 paction and transcriptional control.
